segments.text:
Societal Influences of Vaccines
CLS-N390 Vaccines: Science and Society

L e arning Objectives
Apply the public health approach to investigating a cholera outbreak.
01
Describe t he essential services and stakeholders that make up public health.
02
Describe t he influences of vaccination on the health of a population, economy, and society.
03

What is public health?
01

Assessment
K now what needs to be done
S ystematically collect, analyze, and make information avaiable abo ut what makes a healthy community
Policy Development
Important part of getting things done
Promote the use of a scientific knowledge base in policy and decision making
Essential Services of Public Health
Assurance
Make sure it gets done
E nsure provision of service to those in need
In 1988, the Institute of Medicine defined three core functions of public health agencies
Must be carried out at all levels of government
Essential Public Health Services

Essential Public Health Services
Monitoring Health
Assessment
Example: Monitor smoking use among segments of the population, such as youth
Diagnose & Investigate
Example: Investigate risk factors associated with tobacco use
Evaluate what is considered a “healthy” population – identify what is not a healthy practice in that population
Accurately diagnose individuals (i.e. lab tests, case definitions, etc.) and determine risk factors

Essential Public Health Services
Inform, Educate, Empower
Policy Development
Example: Place public service announcements on television regarding dangers of smoking
Mobilize community partners
Example: Work with advocacy groups to develop antismoking interventions what will work in a specific community
Develop policies and plans
Share knowledge with public!
Work within the community to get message out and identify what will be most effective
Work with policy makers to develop policies, laws, and plans  to benefit the community
Example: Passage of Proposition 99 (the cigarette tax to fund antismoking campaigns); smoke-free workplaces, apartment complexes, and other shared spaces

Essential Public Health Services
Enforce laws and regulations
Assurance
Example: Cigarette vending machines require scanning of identification to purchase
Personal health services
Example: Ads for anti-smoking campaigns broadcast or printed in languages other than English
Implement actions to enforce laws and regulations
Link individuals to needed personal health services – Address potential barriers related to culture, language, and special populations

Essential Public Health Services
Competent workforce
Assurance
Example: Ensure knowledgeable workforce is in place to develop and implement the antismoking campaigns
Evaluate
Example: Evaluate to determine if antismoking actions are going as desired – determine if they might be improved
Research!
Assure a competent public health and personal healthcare workforce
Ongoing evaluation of implemented practices, strategies, laws, policies, etc.
Take insight gained from the field on what works and confirm through collaborative study with health researchers
Function at all levels
Stakeholder Roles in Public Health

The Impact of Vaccines
02

Vaccination Impact on Health – United States
Vaccine | Peak cases in prevaccine era (year) | Vaccine coverage in children 9-35 
months old (%) | Cases in 
2017 | Disease reduction (%)
Smallpox | 110,672 (1920) | -- | 0 | 100
Diphtheria | 30,508 (1936) | 94.0 | 0 | 100
Measles (non-imported) | 763,094 (1958) | 91.5 | 99 | 99.99
Mumps | 212,932 (1964) | 91.5 | 6,109 | 97.13
Rubella | 486,796 (1964) | 91.5 | 7 | 100
Congenital rubella syndrome | 20,000 (1964-65) | -- | 5 | 99.96
Pertussis | 265,269 (1934) | 94.0 | 18,975 | 92.85
Polio (paralytic) | 21,269 (1952) | 92.0 | 9 | 100
Tetanus | 601 (1948) | 94.0 | 33 | 94.51
Vaccination Impact on Health – Global

Vaccination Impact on Health
Herd Immunity – sufficient proportion of the population is vaccinated = transmission of the infecting agent is halted
Protects the unvaccinated – too young, too vulnerable, too immunocompromised
Successful vaccination programs for several infectious diseases
Insufficient distribution of vaccines – prevents the establishment of herd immunity
Prevention of other diseases
Influenza virus – frequently complicated by bacterial pneumonia and otitis media and fungal pneumonia
1918 – secondary bacteria bronchopneumonia ( S. pneumoniae, S. pyogenes, H. influenzae, and S. aureus )
Measles vaccine prevents non-measles illnesses
Measles causes immunologic amnesia by eliminating B cells – leaves survivors susceptible to previously immune diseases for up to 3 years

Vaccine Impact on Economy
Smallpox – eradication cost were over $100 million – cost savings of $13.5 billion annually
Polio – cost savings of $1.5 billion annually

Vaccine Impact on Economy
As population grows and we establish resident closer to other living cultures – greater risk of acquiring infectious diseases through zoonotic transmission
2011 – 2017 there were 1,307 epidemics of infectious diseases globally - $60 billion annually to manage
2014 Ebola epidemic in Libera, Sierra Leone, and Guinea
2015 Zika epidemic in Latin America
What happens when we don’t have a vaccine?
Vaccine Impact on Economy

Vaccination Impact on Society
Combination of poverty, malnutrition, poor hygiene and sanitation, overcrowding, discrimination, and reduced access to health care = underprivileged are disproportionately afflicted by infectious diseases
Expanded Program of Immunization (EPI) – World Health Organization 1974
National and international scale
Great impact to reduce infectious diseases to all – regardless of other disadvantages
Over 25 years – vaccine coverage in developing countries increased from 5% to 80%
Administration of vaccines by UNICEF was deemed so important that there have been several ceasefires in civil conflicts

Vaccine Impact on Society
Inequity seen in those living in poverty has been demonstrated many times
Successful vaccination to infants and children globally requires significant healthcare infrastructure
Scaffolded across national, state, district, and community levels
Promote other important messages and health education – malnutrition, hygiene and sanitation, preventable diseases
Global partnerships can be long lasting and beneficial

Vaccine Impact on Society
Vaccine programs provide degree of social mobility
Poverty and associated ill-health and mortality no longer determine life chances
Recipients have potential for improved life expectancy
Vaccination  in elderly prevent infections with high mortality and morbidity – influenza, pneumococcus, herpes zoster, pertussis
Vaccines prior to travel
Provides opportunity for safe discovery of new cultures and environments
Mass movements of refugees
Global mass culture or religious gatherings

A Public Health Approach to Cholera
03
Just a reminder…

London – early 1800s
Cholera (fatal intestinal disease) was rampant
Caused death to tens of thousands of people within hours of first symptoms
Before bacteria and viruses were recognized as cause of disease
Popular opinion = cholera caused by bad air coming from rotten organic matter
John Snow (British physician) had different ideas
Believed illness was spread by contaminated water supply
Sewage was routinely dumped into the Thames River and cesspools near town
Worked to trace source of cholera outbreak
Considered father of modern epidemiology – changed the way we look at disease and how they spread

Snow conducted public health surveillance
Assisted by local clergyman, Reverend Henry Whitehead
Tracked down and interviewed cholera victims and families
Recorded deaths from cholera on a map of London
Saw a large cluster of cases were occurring in a specific area
What is the problem?
These efforts highlight the benefits of linking scientific inquiry with engagement of community stakeholders and shared ownership for health.

Snow examined data to identify risk factors
Tried to determine cause of death by using patterns of where cases were occurring
Based on previous work, he believed waster was a potential cholera source
Marked all water pumps on map
Prompted him to identify where people drew their water from
What is the cause?

After identifying likely risk factors  evaluate potential interventions
Risk factor = water supply from Broad Street pump
Snow understood the interventions required control the cholera outbreak needed to
Stop exposure to the contaminated water supply (i.e. Broad Street pump)
Stop exposure to the entire supply of contaminated water in the area
What works?

Intervention implemented – removal of handle from Broad Street pump
Prevented people from drawing water
Took a long battle with politicians and water companies…
Snow convinced British government that water contaminated with sewage was the source of cholera and other diseases
Resulted in implementation of policies and laws for water sanitation
How do you do it?
